items.passage:
Oral tradition refers to the system of cultural knowledge that a community transmits across generations through spoken performance rather than written text. Songs, genealogies, instructional tales, and origin narratives constitute the principal vehicles. Within nonliterate societies, these forms are not regarded as informal supplements to memory; they are treated as the primary repository of knowledge, anchored by formulaic phrases that help reciters reconstruct long sequences with significant accuracy.

This reliance on memory is sustained by a set of techniques that have been carefully refined by specialists known as oral historians. Many performances depend on rhythm and rhyme, which constrain how a line may unfold and therefore reduce the burden on recall. Repetition is also exploited: a single image, such as a battle or a river crossing, may be revisited several times within a single narrative. Audience participation reinforces the structure further. Listeners often interject expected refrains, which makes deviation immediately noticeable to the community and helps stabilize the version that survives.

Despite this internal discipline, oral tradition is not static. Performers adjust details to suit the occasion, the audience, or shifting political circumstances. A king newly enthroned may find genealogies subtly extended in his favor, while a famine year may bring older tales of resource sharing back into circulation. Such flexibility, while frustrating to historians who expect a single authoritative version, has been described by recent ethnographers as the very feature that keeps oral knowledge socially relevant rather than archived. The tradition retains weight because the community continues to use it.
paragraphs: Oral tradition refers to the system of cultural knowledge that a community transmits across generations through spoken performance rather than written text. Songs, genealogies, instructional tales, and origin narratives constitute the principal vehicles. Within nonliterate societies, these forms are not regarded as informal supplements to memory; they are treated as the primary repository of knowledge, anchored by formulaic phrases that help reciters reconstruct long sequences with significant accuracy.

This reliance on memory is sustained by a set of techniques that have been carefully refined by specialists known as oral historians. Many performances depend on rhythm and rhyme, which constrain how a line may unfold and therefore reduce the burden on recall. Repetition is also exploited: a single image, such as a battle or a river crossing, may be revisited several times within a single narrative. Audience participation reinforces the structure further. Listeners often interject expected refrains, which makes deviation immediately noticeable to the community and helps stabilize the version that survives.

Despite this internal discipline, oral tradition is not static. Performers adjust details to suit the occasion, the audience, or shifting political circumstances. A king newly enthroned may find genealogies subtly extended in his favor, while a famine year may bring older tales of resource sharing back into circulation. Such flexibility, while frustrating to historians who expect a single authoritative version, has been described by recent ethnographers as the very feature that keeps oral knowledge socially relevant rather than archived. The tradition retains weight because the community continues to use it.

questions.stem: The word "constitute" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: challenge
options.B: make up
options.C: replace
options.D: limit

questions.explanation: In context, the sentence states that songs, genealogies, and tales "constitute the principal vehicles" of oral tradition — meaning they together form or make up those vehicles.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why is repetition used in oral performances?

options.A: It allows performers to shorten the overall narrative.
options.B: It signals that a different reciter has taken over.
options.C: It revisits key images several times within a story.
options.D: It introduces variations preferred by younger listeners.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explicitly states that a single image "may be revisited several times within a single narrative," identifying revisiting as the function of repetition.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about audience participation?

options.A: Departures from the expected version are quickly recognized.
options.B: Audiences usually remain silent during oral performances.
options.C: Audiences gradually replace the original performers over time.
options.D: Refrains were introduced to oral tradition only recently.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says listener interjections make "deviation immediately noticeable to the community," implying that audiences serve as a check on accuracy.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraph 2?

options.A: It provides further evidence that supports the techniques outlined in paragraph 2.
options.B: It rejects the central description of oral tradition given in paragraph 2.
options.C: It qualifies paragraph 2 by showing that disciplined performance still allows adjustment.
options.D: It contrasts oral tradition with the written records mentioned in paragraph 2.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 opens with "Despite this internal discipline," signaling that the rigorous techniques of paragraph 2 coexist with situational flexibility — a qualification rather than a contradiction.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention a newly enthroned king in paragraph 3?

options.A: To prove that oral tradition is less reliable than written archives.
options.B: To suggest that political figures usually rejected oral histories.
options.C: To illustrate the early origins of genealogies in royal courts.
options.D: To illustrate how performers adjust details to fit current circumstances.

questions.explanation: The king example demonstrates the broader claim made in paragraph 3 that performers adjust details to suit shifting political circumstances.

items.passage:
Modern telecommunications rest on the principle that information of any kind — speech, text, or images — can be converted into a stream of discrete electrical or optical pulses. This insight, developed during the middle of the twentieth century, transformed long-distance communication from an analog process subject to noise into a digital one capable of error correction. The earliest practical application appeared in trunk telephone lines, where engineers compressed voice signals into binary code to multiply the number of calls that a single cable could carry.

The same principle was later extended to fiber optic transmission, which now carries the bulk of intercontinental traffic. In a fiber system, the binary stream modulates a laser, and the resulting pulses travel through extremely pure glass strands at velocities limited only by the refractive properties of the medium. Because light pulses experience comparatively little attenuation, intermediate amplifiers can be spaced tens of kilometers apart. This spacing has been steadily increased as fiber purity has improved, lowering both maintenance costs and signal latency.

Despite these advances, telecommunications engineers face a continuing problem of capacity. As users demand higher resolution video and more interactive services, the volume of pulses required has grown rapidly. One response has involved sending several wavelengths of light through a single fiber simultaneously, a technique known as wavelength-division multiplexing. Another approach relies on more efficient encoding, allowing each pulse to carry information about multiple bits. Both strategies push fiber networks closer to a theoretical ceiling set by the physics of the glass itself, suggesting that future gains may require entirely new transmission media rather than further refinement of existing ones.

Because light pulses experience comparatively little attenuation, intermediate amplifiers can be spaced tens of kilometers apart. This spacing has been steadily increased as fiber purity has improved, lowering both maintenance costs and signal latency.

As users demand higher resolution video and more interactive services, the volume of pulses required has grown rapidly. One response has involved sending several wavelengths of light through a single fiber simultaneously, a technique known as wavelength-division multiplexing. Another approach relies on more efficient encoding, allowing each pulse to carry information about multiple bits. Both strategies push fiber networks closer to a theoretical ceiling set by the physics of the glass itself, suggesting that future gains may require entirely new transmission media rather than further refinement of existing ones.

questions.stem: The word "attenuation" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: weakening
options.B: reflection
options.C: warming
options.D: distortion

questions.explanation: The sentence explains that because pulses experience little attenuation, amplifiers can be widely spaced — implying attenuation refers to loss or weakening of the signal.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what change did the conversion of signals into pulses bring to telephone trunk lines?

options.A: It removed the need for any amplifying equipment.
options.B: It increased the number of calls each cable could carry.
options.C: It introduced satellites as a new transmission medium.
options.D: It changed the analog signals back into spoken speech.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that engineers compressed voice into binary code "to multiply the number of calls that a single cable could carry."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It contradicts the digital model that paragraph 1 introduces.
options.B: It returns to analog techniques abandoned in paragraph 1.
options.C: It compares fiber transmission to satellite-based systems.
options.D: It extends the principle from paragraph 1 to a newer medium.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 opens with "The same principle was later extended to fiber optic transmission," signaling continuity and extension rather than contrast.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the spacing of amplifiers?

options.A: The cost of laser equipment continues to rise.
options.B: Older copper systems no longer need any amplifiers.
options.C: Improvements in fiber purity have reduced maintenance demands.
options.D: Engineers prefer satellites to fiber for short routes.

questions.explanation: The text says spacing increases as fiber purity improves, "lowering both maintenance costs and signal latency," so improved purity reduces how often equipment must be maintained.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, all of the following are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Wavelength-division multiplexing sends several light wavelengths through one fiber.
options.B: Some encoding methods let each pulse carry multiple bits of information.
options.C: Engineers have already removed the theoretical ceiling on fiber capacity.
options.D: Demand for higher resolution video has increased the volume of pulses.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states that current strategies push networks toward a theoretical ceiling and that future gains may require new media — meaning the ceiling has not been removed.

items.passage:
The network of trade routes commonly grouped under the name "Silk Road" operated for more than fifteen centuries across the inner reaches of Eurasia. Although the name suggests a single highway dedicated to silk, the system was in fact a loose constellation of caravan paths, river crossings, and oasis stops that shifted with political conditions. Goods such as silk, paper, glassware, and spices moved across thousands of kilometers in relay fashion, exchanged at each major node rather than carried end to end by any single merchant.

This relay structure had significant implications for the cities that grew along the network. Towns like Samarkand and Dunhuang prospered not merely as marketplaces but as centers of credit, translation, and craft production. Local rulers competed to provide caravanserais — fortified inns where merchants and animals could rest under guard — and to maintain reliable water sources between stops. Because no single empire ever controlled the full length of the corridor, traders relied on overlapping legal customs and on personal networks of trust, often built across generations of families.

The Silk Road also served as a conduit for ideas and technologies whose movement is harder to quantify than that of trade goods. Buddhism reached eastern Asia from northern India along these routes, while paper manufacturing techniques moved westward from China toward the Mediterranean basin. Astronomers, physicians, and mathematicians exchanged texts at the major hubs, and disease vectors traveled with them. Some scholars argue that the breadth and durability of this exchange depended less on any one ruler's protection than on the fact that small commercial actors had powerful incentives to keep the system functioning.

questions.stem: The word "constellation" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: assortment
options.B: ranking
options.C: scheme
options.D: circle

questions.explanation: In context, the Silk Road is described as "a loose constellation of caravan paths," indicating a varied collection or assortment rather than a single organized highway.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why did traders rely on personal networks of trust?

options.A: Because most caravanserais charged unpredictable fees.
options.B: Because no single empire ruled the entire corridor.
options.C: Because written contracts were forbidden by local rulers.
options.D: Because most traders dealt only in perishable goods.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explicitly states that traders depended on overlapping legal customs and personal trust networks "because no single empire ever controlled the full length of the corridor."

questions.stem: What is the function of paragraph 1 in the passage?

options.A: It corrects a common misconception about the Silk Road's structure.
options.B: It compares the Silk Road to other ancient overland networks.
options.C: It introduces evidence later refuted in paragraph 3 of the passage.
options.D: It argues that the Silk Road was less important than once believed.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 acknowledges that the name "Silk Road" implies a single highway and then corrects this view by describing the routes as a loose, shifting network.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention caravanserais in paragraph 2?

options.A: To prove that long-distance trade collapsed without imperial backing.
options.B: To suggest that Silk Road merchants disliked unfamiliar locations.
options.C: To illustrate how local rulers competed to support passing trade.
options.D: To explain how families divided inheritances along the corridor.

questions.explanation: Caravanserais are introduced as evidence that "local rulers competed" to attract and protect passing merchants, showing the local incentives that supported the network.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, all of the following moved along the Silk Road EXCEPT:

options.A: techniques for manufacturing paper
options.B: the religion of Buddhism
options.C: diseases carried by travelers
options.D: a unified imperial legal code

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 mentions paper, Buddhism, and diseases as items that moved along the corridor, but the passage explicitly notes the absence of a single empire and unified law.

items.passage:
Light travels in straight lines through a uniform medium, but when it crosses a boundary into a medium of different density, its path bends. This bending, known as refraction, occurs because light moves more slowly through denser materials such as glass or water than through air. The amount of bending depends on the relative densities of the two media and on the angle at which the light strikes the boundary.

This behavior was first formalized in the seventeenth century by the Dutch astronomer Willebrord Snell, who showed that for any pair of materials a fixed ratio describes how light bends as it crosses between them. The same principle explains everyday observations: a straight stick appears bent at the surface of a pond, and a pool of water seems shallower than it actually is. Because of refraction, light from objects underwater arrives at the eye from an angle that does not correspond to the true position of the source.

Refraction is also central to the design of lenses. A lens is a transparent disc whose two surfaces are curved so that light entering at slightly different angles is bent by slightly different amounts. By controlling the curvature, an optical designer can cause parallel light rays to converge at a single point, called the focal point. Telescopes, microscopes, and corrective eyeglasses all rely on this geometry, although each application demands a different combination of focal length and image clarity. Modern manufacturers grind lenses to tolerances measured in fractions of a wavelength, allowing instruments to approach the resolution limits set by the wave nature of light itself.

Because of refraction, light from objects underwater arrives at the eye from an angle that does not correspond to the true position of the source.

Modern manufacturers grind lenses to tolerances measured in fractions of a wavelength, allowing instruments to approach the resolution limits set by the wave nature of light itself.

questions.stem: The word "converge" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: spread
options.B: reflect
options.C: meet
options.D: scatter

questions.explanation: The sentence describes parallel rays being bent so they "converge at a single point," indicating that the rays come together — meet — at the focal point.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what determines the amount of bending when light enters a new medium?

options.A: Only the brightness of the light source.
options.B: Only the color of the entering light.
options.C: The relative densities and the angle of entry.
options.D: The age of the boundary material.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that the bending depends on "the relative densities of the two media and on the angle at which the light strikes the boundary."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about an object viewed underwater?

options.A: It always appears the same as it would on land.
options.B: It appears in a position different from its real one.
options.C: It cannot be seen from above the surface.
options.D: It seems to be located much deeper than it is.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains that light from underwater objects reaches the eye from an angle that does not match the source, implying that the apparent position differs from the actual position.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraphs 1 and 2?

options.A: It questions the basic principle of refraction described earlier.
options.B: It returns to examples of refraction in nature only.
options.C: It replaces refraction with an alternative explanation of vision.
options.D: It applies the principle of refraction to a practical technology.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 takes the refraction principle established in the earlier paragraphs and shows how it underlies the design and use of lenses.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention a stick appearing bent at the surface of a pond?

options.A: To illustrate the everyday consequences of refraction.
options.B: To suggest that Snell's law was based on careless observation.
options.C: To compare optical instruments with the unaided eye.
options.D: To argue that water distorts color rather than position.

questions.explanation: The bent-stick image is offered as an everyday observation that the principle of refraction explains, supporting the broader claim in paragraph 2.

items.passage:
Slow sand filtration is a water purification technique in which untreated water is allowed to percolate downward through a thick bed of fine sand, leaving behind microorganisms, suspended particles, and dissolved organic matter. Unlike rapid filtration systems, which depend on chemical coagulants and high flow rates, slow sand filters rely on a biological layer that develops naturally on the top few centimeters of the sand bed. This layer, known as the schmutzdecke, performs most of the actual purification work.

The schmutzdecke is a complex living community of bacteria, protozoa, and tiny invertebrates that gradually colonize the sand surface during the first few weeks of operation. As untreated water passes through this layer, suspended particles are trapped mechanically while dissolved organic compounds are consumed by resident microorganisms. The biological layer also produces enzymes that break down harmful pathogens. Once mature, the schmutzdecke can remove more than ninety-nine percent of bacteria and many viruses, achieving water quality comparable to chemically treated supplies without the use of synthetic additives.

Slow sand filtration has retained an important role in regions where energy and chemical supplies are unreliable. Several major European cities continue to use the technique for parts of their drinking water supply, and small rural systems across South Asia and East Africa have adopted versions of the method. However, slow sand filters require careful upkeep: the top layer must be skimmed off and the sand exposed to air periodically, and the system cannot handle sudden surges in turbid water. Engineers therefore tend to combine slow sand filtration with pre-treatment steps, especially in watersheds where rainfall events can rapidly load the incoming water with sediment.

This layer, known as the schmutzdecke, performs most of the actual purification work.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The chemical coagulants used in rapid water purification systems.
options.B: How slow sand filtration purifies water and the conditions it requires.
options.C: The geography of water shortages in South Asia and East Africa.
options.D: The history of European drinking water regulations and policies.

questions.explanation: All three paragraphs describe how slow sand filtration works, the biological layer that makes it effective, and the conditions for using it — a focus that only B captures fully.

questions.stem: The word "percolate" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: evaporate
options.B: freeze
options.C: boil
options.D: trickle

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 describes water being allowed to percolate downward through a sand bed; in this context the verb refers to slow filtration or trickling through the sand.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what role do resident microorganisms play in the schmutzdecke?

options.A: They consume dissolved organic compounds carried in the water.
options.B: They produce the sand that lines the bottom of the filter bed.
options.C: They increase the flow rate through the filter substantially.
options.D: They add chemical coagulants to support rapid filtration.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states directly that "dissolved organic compounds are consumed by resident microorganisms" as the water passes through the layer.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about watersheds with heavy rainfall events?

options.A: Such watersheds rarely use slow sand filters in any form.
options.B: Slow sand filters there generally need additional pre-treatment.
options.C: Rainfall events have no measurable effect on water turbidity.
options.D: European cities have stopped using slow sand filters entirely.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says engineers combine slow sand filtration with pre-treatment "especially in watersheds where rainfall events can rapidly load the incoming water with sediment," implying these watersheds need extra steps.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraph 2?

options.A: It contradicts the biological description offered in paragraph 2.
options.B: It repeats paragraph 2 using slightly different vocabulary.
options.C: It moves from how the system works to where and how it is used.
options.D: It explains the original discovery of the schmutzdecke layer.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains the internal biology of the filter, while paragraph 3 shifts to its geographic adoption and the practical conditions under which it is deployed.
